title: Shokupan
subtitle: Japanese milk bread loaf with a tangzhong water roux
excerpt: Japanese sandwich bread made with a tangzhong water roux for an exceptionally soft, pillowy crumb that stays fresh for days longer than a standard enriched loaf. Baked in a covered tin for a straight-sided square slice.
sourceNotes: Shokupan (食パン, literally 'eating bread') is the Japanese standard white sandwich loaf developed during the Meiji period (1868-1912) from Western-influenced baking. The tangzhong technique (water roux, or yu-dane method in Japanese) of cooking a small portion of the flour and water before adding it to the dough was documented in Japanese baking guides from the mid-twentieth century and later popularised in English-language baking writing by Yvonne Chen, 65°C Tangzhong Bread (2007).

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice before freezing. Toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Stays fresh for 3-4 days wrapped at room temperature — notably longer than a standard loaf.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: 180°C conventional / 160°C fan (baked with lid on for the first 20 minutes, then lid off to colour)

glossaryTerms.definition: A water roux made by cooking flour and water (or milk) together to around 65°C until the mixture thickens to a paste. When incorporated into bread dough, the pre-gelatinised starch in the tangzhong retains extra moisture during baking, producing a softer, more pillowy crumb that stays fresh significantly longer than a standard loaf.

content.text: Shokupan's extraordinary softness comes from the

: a small portion of flour cooked with milk to a thick paste before being added to the main dough. The pre-gelatinised starch in the tangzhong absorbs extra water and holds it through the bake, giving the crumb its characteristic pillow-soft texture. The covered tin bake produces the straight sides and square cross-section of a traditional shokupan loaf.

Whisk 25 g {{strong-white-flour}} and 125 ml {{whole-milk}} together in a small saucepan until smooth. Cook over a medium-low heat, stirring constantly, until the mixture thickens to a paste and pulls away from the sides of the pan (about 65°C if you have a thermometer). Remove from the heat and leave to cool to room temperature.
Mix, knead, and bulk ferment, 1 hour

Combine 315 g {{strong-white-flour}}, {{fast-action-yeast}}, {{fine-salt}}, and {{caster-sugar}} in a large bowl. Add the cooled tangzhong, lukewarm {{whole-milk}}, and {{whole-eggs}}. Mix until a rough dough forms, then add the {{unsalted-butter}} and knead by hand for 12 minutes or in a stand mixer for 8 minutes until smooth, elastic, and slightly sticky. Cover and bulk ferment until doubled, about 1 hour.

Grease a 900 g loaf tin. Divide the dough into four equal pieces. Roll each piece into a ball, then roll each ball into a rough oval. Fold the sides in and roll up into a cylinder. Place the four cylinders side by side in the tin, seam-side down. Cover and prove until the dough domes 2 cm above the rim of the tin, about 1 hour. If you have a lidded Pullman tin, fit the lid on when the dough reaches the rim.

Heat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan). Place the covered tin (or cover with greased foil) in the oven and bake for 20 minutes. Remove the lid or foil and bake for a further 15-18 minutes until deep golden. The loaf should read 94-96°C internally. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a rack and cool completely before slicing.
